3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

agentic ai coverBuilding agentic DevOps workflows taught me something I didn’t expect: the real skill isn’t prompting an AI to do more. It’s teaching it exactly where to stop.
Most people think working with AI agents is about speed, getting more done faster, with less typing. That’s not what this was about.
I stopped treating Claude Code like an assistant and started treating it like a system I had to design.
The Moment It Clicked
I asked Claude to add a React component to my static HTML/CSS site. Simple request. I expected code back.
Instead, Claude refused and explained exactly why: my project’s CLAUDE.md file explicitly states "No JavaScript, no framework, no build step." Claude wasn't being difficult. It was following a rule I'd written myself, before I ever asked.
That’s the moment agentic AI stopped feeling like a chatbot and started feeling like a colleague who actually reads the documentation, every time, without fail, without ego.
Lesson: an AI agent isn’t naturally aligned with your project. You have to define the rules of engagement-in writing-before it can be trusted with real decisions.
Turning Repetition Into Reusable Power
Every DevOps engineer knows the pain of typing the same detailed prompt over and over. I built a skill-a single slash command, /scaffold-terraform, that generates a full, production-shaped Terraform setup on demand: S3, CloudFront, OAC, variables, outputs, providers, backend. One command. No manual code.
Skills turn tribal knowledge into infrastructure. What used to live only in my head now lives in a command anyone on my team could run the same way, every time.
Specialists, Not Generalists
The most counterintuitive lesson came from subagents. I built three: a security auditor, a cost optimizer, and a Terraform writer -and each one is configured completely differently on purpose.

• The security auditor can read and report -but it can’t write. Ever. Its job is to flag risk, not fix it unsupervised.

• The cost optimizer runs on a smaller, faster model because scanning for cost inefficiencies is pattern-matching, not deep reasoning.

• The Terraform writer inherits whatever model powers the main session because generating real infrastructure code deserves real capability.
This is the same principle every mature engineering org already lives by: access should match responsibility, not convenience. Agentic AI doesn’t change that rule. It just makes you actually enforce it, in config, in writing.
Giving the Agent Real Hands
Then came MCP, the protocol that let Claude actually reach into GitHub and query my repositories directly, instead of me running commands on its behalf.
Getting there wasn’t clean. I hit config mismatches, wrong file paths, a tool switch mid-way when my subscription ran out, and even a scare when a token nearly got exposed in a screenshot. But once it connected, the difference was obvious: Claude wasn’t just suggesting actions anymore. It was taking them inside boundaries I’d already set.
The Guardrails Matter More Than the Agent
The part I didn’t expect to care about this much: hooks.
I wrote a rule that blocks terraform destroy and rm -rf At the command level, not because I don't trust Claude, but because I don't trust any system, human or AI, with irreversible actions unless there's a second gate in place.
That’s the real mindset shift agentic AI forces on you: safety isn’t something you bolt on after the agent misbehaves. It’s a boundary you build before you ever hand over the keys.
The Real Takeaway
My thinking fully flipped.
I stopped asking, “How do I get Claude to do more?”
I started asking, “What exactly should Claude be allowed to do, and what should it never touch without me?”
That question -not the code- is the actual skill behind agentic DevOps.
